title: Dividend ETFs See Record Inflows as Investors Flee the Selloff — SCHD Leads the Pack
description: With the S&P 500 in correction territory, investors are piling into dividend ETFs like SCHD, VYM, and DVY. Here's where the money is going and why quality dividends are the new safe haven.
lede: As the S&P 500 extends its losses amid the Hormuz crisis, tariff uncertainty, and tech liability concerns, investors are doing what they always do in turbulent markets: rotating into dividend-paying assets. The Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) — the most popular dividend ETF in America with $60 billion in assets — just went ex-dividend on March 25, paying $0.2569 per share while the broader market fell another 1.7%.

faqs.q: What is SCHD and why is it popular during selloffs?
faqs.a: SCHD (Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF) tracks 100 US stocks with 10+ years of consecutive dividend payments. It charges only 0.06% in fees, yields 3.47%, and holds quality companies like Broadcom, Coca-Cola, and Chevron. During selloffs, investors flock to SCHD because dividend-paying stocks tend to be less volatile and provide income regardless of share price.

faqs.q: How much does SCHD pay in dividends?
faqs.a: SCHD pays approximately $1.06 per share annually in quarterly distributions, yielding about 3.47% at its current price of $30.44. The most recent payment was $0.2569 per share on March 30, 2026.

faqs.q: Is SCHD better than the S&P 500 during a bear market?
faqs.a: Historically, dividend-focused ETFs like SCHD have experienced smaller drawdowns than the S&P 500 during corrections. During the 2022 bear market, SCHD declined roughly 5% while the S&P 500 fell over 19%. However, SCHD typically underperforms in strong bull markets driven by growth stocks.

faqs.q: What are the best dividend ETFs to buy during a market correction?
faqs.a: The most popular dividend ETFs by assets are SCHD ($60B), VYM ($55B), DVY ($20B), HDV ($10B), and DGRO ($28B). SCHD and DGRO emphasize dividend growth quality, while VYM and DVY focus more on current yield. Each has different sector weightings and selection criteria.

sections.heading: The Rotation Into Quality Dividends
paragraphs: When markets sell off, money doesn't disappear — it moves. And right now, it's moving into dividend ETFs at a pace not seen since the 2022 correction. SCHD, VYM, DVY, and HDV have all seen elevated inflows as investors seek assets that pay them to wait out the volatility.

The logic is straightforward: if you're holding stocks that are dropping 10-20%, you'd rather hold stocks that are dropping 5% and paying you 3.5% in dividends. SCHD's quality screen — requiring 10 consecutive years of dividend payments before a stock is even eligible — naturally filters out speculative companies that tend to get hit hardest in downturns.

sections.heading: Why SCHD at $60 Billion
paragraphs: SCHD has become the default dividend ETF for a reason. At 0.06% expense ratio, you pay $6 per year on a $10,000 investment. The Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index it tracks doesn't just screen for yield — it ranks eligible stocks by cash flow to debt ratio, return on equity, dividend yield, and 5-year dividend growth rate. The result is a portfolio of companies that can actually afford their dividends.

Current top holdings include Broadcom, Coca-Cola, Verizon, Cisco, Chevron, PepsiCo, and Home Depot — a diversified mix across sectors with no single stock dominating. Compare that to the S&P 500, where the top 10 stocks account for over 35% of the index.
Dive into SCHD's complete dividend history, run income projections, and compare it against VYM, DVY, HDV, and DGRO with our SCHD Dividend Analysis — updated with every quarterly distribution.

sections.heading: The Dividend Growth Story
paragraphs: SCHD's quarterly distribution has grown from about $0.17 per share in 2021 to $0.27 in 2025 — roughly 60% growth in four years. That's the real bull case for quality dividend ETFs: you're not just getting income today, you're getting growing income. If SCHD maintains even a modest 5% annual dividend growth rate, the yield on cost for today's buyers will be above 5% within a decade.

This is why long-term investors are using the current selloff as a buying opportunity rather than a reason to panic. A 10% drop in SCHD's price means your $10,000 investment buys more shares, which means more dividends, which compounds faster when the market recovers.

sections.heading: Where Dividend ETFs Fit in a Crisis Portfolio
paragraphs: Dividend ETFs aren't a substitute for bonds or cash in a true crisis — they're still equities and they still fall. But they fall less, recover faster, and pay you while you wait. In the 2022 correction, SCHD's total return (including dividends) outperformed the S&P 500 by roughly 15 percentage points.

The smart allocation during uncertainty: keep emergency cash, hold core bonds for stability, and use dividend ETFs like SCHD as the equity anchor. You're essentially getting paid 3.5% annually to own high-quality businesses while waiting for the growth stocks to find their floor.
Compare SCHD against individual dividend payers using our monthly dividend stocks list, or model long-term compound growth with the DRIP calculator.
